items.title: Overview
items.body: The Acme Design System provides a shared library of reusable components, design tokens, and patterns that enable product teams to build consistent, accessible user interfaces across web, iOS, and Android platforms. It encodes Acme's design language into a single source of truth so that designers and engineers work from the same specifications.

items.description: When building a new product or feature within the Acme product suite that must conform to the Acme brand and interaction standards.
When an existing product is being redesigned or migrated to a modern front-end stack and needs a consistent component library.
When contributing a new component or pattern that will be shared across multiple Acme products.
When building a white-label product that must not carry the Acme brand identity.

alternative.identifier: custom theme or independent design system
alternative.rationale: The Acme Design System encodes Acme-specific brand decisions (color palette, typography, iconography). White-label products require a neutral foundation that can be re-skinned per customer. Use the token architecture as inspiration, but do not depend on Acme-branded tokens directly.

items.description: When building a one-off marketing landing page with highly custom, editorial layouts that intentionally break grid and component conventions.

alternative.rationale: Marketing pages often require creative freedom that a component system constrains. Use the brand color tokens and typography scale for consistency, but do not force editorial layouts into rigid component patterns.

items.guidance: Use semantic tokens instead of raw color, spacing, or typography values in all production code.
items.rationale: Semantic tokens abstract the intent (e.g., 'color-text-primary') from the value (e.g., '#1a1a1a'). This enables theming, dark mode, and system-wide visual changes from a single source of truth without touching component code.

items.guidance: Do not modify or override design token values at the component level. Use the token as-is or request a new token.
items.rationale: Overriding tokens breaks the contract between the design system and consuming products. When the system updates a token value, overridden components will not pick up the change, creating visual drift.

items.guidance: Test all components at 200% browser zoom to ensure content does not overflow, overlap, or become inaccessible.
items.rationale: WCAG 1.4.4 requires that content remains functional at 200% text size. Components that break at zoom levels below 200% fail this criterion and exclude users with low vision.

items.guidance: Prefer composition over customization. Combine existing components to build new patterns rather than adding configuration props to existing components.
items.rationale: Every prop added to a component increases its API surface, testing burden, and cognitive load. Composing simple components into larger patterns keeps each component focused and predictable.

items.guidance: Every interactive component must be fully operable with a keyboard alone — no mouse or touch required.
items.rationale: Keyboard operability is the foundation of accessibility. Users who rely on switch devices, screen readers, or alternative input methods all depend on keyboard support as the lowest common denominator.

items.guidance: Pin the design system package version in production applications. Upgrade deliberately, not automatically.
items.rationale: Automatic upgrades can introduce visual regressions or breaking API changes. Pinning versions ensures stability while allowing teams to upgrade on their own schedule after testing.

items.guidance: Do not wrap design system components in additional DOM elements that alter their semantics or accessibility tree.
items.rationale: Wrapping a button in a div with an onClick handler, or nesting a link inside a button, creates unexpected behavior for assistive technology. Use components as designed — their semantic structure is intentional.

items.guidance: When contributing a new component, provide at minimum: a purpose block, an API spec, and usage guidelines before publishing to production.
items.rationale: Components without documentation are components without a contract. Consumers cannot use them correctly if the intended use cases, API, and constraints are not documented.

entityGroups.name: Acme Design System — Starter Kit
entityGroups.description: A representative sample of components, tokens, a foundation, and a pattern demonstrating DSDS document structure.

entities.name: Button
entities.description: A clickable element that triggers an action. Buttons are the primary mechanism for submitting forms, opening dialogs, confirming decisions, and initiating operations.

lastUpdated.note: Added focus-visible guidance and tightened tap-target language.

items.name: Emphasis
items.description: The visual weight of the button, indicating the relative importance of the action.

values.description: The highest-emphasis button. Used for the single most important action on the surface.
A medium-emphasis button with a visible border. Used alongside a primary button for secondary actions.
A low-emphasis button with no border or background. Used for tertiary actions in dense layouts.

items.name: Size
items.description: Controls padding, font size, and minimum touch target area.

values.description: Compact size for dense layouts such as toolbars and table rows.
Large size for hero surfaces and mobile-first layouts.

items.name: Default
items.description: The resting state of the button.

items.name: Hover
items.description: Visual feedback when the pointer is over the button.

items.name: Focus
items.description: Visible focus ring when the button receives keyboard focus.

items.name: Disabled
items.description: Non-interactive state. The button is visible but cannot be activated.
When the user needs to trigger an action such as submitting a form, saving data, or opening a dialog.
When a destructive action needs to be initiated, such as deleting a record. Pair with a confirmation dialog.
When the action navigates to a different page or URL.

alternative.rationale: Links carry native navigation semantics. Screen readers announce them as links, and browsers support open-in-new-tab.

items.guidance: Limit each surface to one primary button.
items.rationale: Multiple primary buttons dilute visual hierarchy.

items.guidance: Use the loading state instead of disabling the button during async operations.
items.rationale: A disabled button gives no feedback that an action is in progress.

items.guidance: When using an icon-only button, provide an aria-label.
items.rationale: Screen readers need a text equivalent for every interactive control.

agentDocumentBlocks.purpose: Trigger a user-initiated action within the current view without causing page navigation.

items.description: Use button for in-page actions; use link for navigation to a URL.
Use button when a visible text label is present; use icon-button for icon-only affordances.

items.guidance: Do not use for navigating to a different page or URL.
items.rationale: Buttons signal actions and links signal navigation. Assistive technology announces each differently, and users rely on that distinction to predict what a control will do.

items.guidance: Limit each surface to one primary-emphasis button.
items.rationale: A single primary action keeps the visual hierarchy clear. When several buttons compete at primary emphasis, the intended next step becomes ambiguous.

items.guidance: Always provide an accessible label via visible text or aria-label.
items.rationale: Icon-only buttons without an accessible name are unusable with screen readers and fail WCAG 4.1.2 (Name, Role, Value).

items.guidance: Using a button to navigate to another page.
items.rationale: Instead: Use a link element with href.

items.guidance: Placing multiple primary buttons on the same surface.
items.rationale: Instead: Use one primary and one or more secondary or tertiary buttons.

entities.name: Link
entities.description: A navigational element that takes the user to a different page or URL.

items.description: When the action navigates the user to a different page, section, or external resource.
When the action triggers a state change or submits data without navigation.

alternative.rationale: Buttons have action semantics. Using a link for non-navigation actions creates a mismatch between role and behavior.

items.guidance: Always pair link color with an underline for inline links within body text.
items.rationale: Color alone is insufficient to distinguish links for users with color vision deficiency.

agentDocumentBlocks.purpose: Trigger a user-initiated action within the current view without causing page navigation.

items.description: Use button for in-page actions; use link for navigation to a URL.
Use button when a visible text label is present; use icon-button for icon-only affordances.

items.guidance: Do not use for navigating to a different page or URL.
items.rationale: Buttons signal actions and links signal navigation. Assistive technology announces each differently, and users rely on that distinction to predict what a control will do.

items.guidance: Limit each surface to one primary-emphasis button.
items.rationale: A single primary action keeps the visual hierarchy clear. When several buttons compete at primary emphasis, the intended next step becomes ambiguous.

items.guidance: Always provide an accessible label via visible text or aria-label.
items.rationale: Icon-only buttons without an accessible name are unusable with screen readers and fail WCAG 4.1.2 (Name, Role, Value).

items.guidance: Using a button to navigate to another page.
items.rationale: Instead: Use a link element with href.

items.guidance: Placing multiple primary buttons on the same surface.
items.rationale: Instead: Use one primary and one or more secondary or tertiary buttons.

entities.name: Alert
entities.description: A message bar that communicates status, errors, warnings, or informational messages to the user.

items.description: When a system condition needs the user's attention — an error, a warning, or a status change that affects their work.
When confirming a routine successful action — quiet inline feedback keeps attention on the task; reserve alerts for conditions that need action.

items.name: Severity
items.description: The visual treatment and semantic meaning of the alert.

values.description: Warns about a potential issue that doesn't block the user.
Communicates an error that requires attention.

items.guidance: Use role='alert' or aria-live='assertive' for error alerts so screen readers announce them immediately.
items.rationale: Without a live region, dynamically injected error messages are invisible to assistive technology.

agentDocumentBlocks.purpose: Trigger a user-initiated action within the current view without causing page navigation.

items.description: Use button for in-page actions; use link for navigation to a URL.
Use button when a visible text label is present; use icon-button for icon-only affordances.

items.guidance: Do not use for navigating to a different page or URL.
items.rationale: Buttons signal actions and links signal navigation. Assistive technology announces each differently, and users rely on that distinction to predict what a control will do.

items.guidance: Limit each surface to one primary-emphasis button.
items.rationale: A single primary action keeps the visual hierarchy clear. When several buttons compete at primary emphasis, the intended next step becomes ambiguous.

items.guidance: Always provide an accessible label via visible text or aria-label.
items.rationale: Icon-only buttons without an accessible name are unusable with screen readers and fail WCAG 4.1.2 (Name, Role, Value).

items.guidance: Using a button to navigate to another page.
items.rationale: Instead: Use a link element with href.

items.guidance: Placing multiple primary buttons on the same surface.
items.rationale: Instead: Use one primary and one or more secondary or tertiary buttons.

children.description: The primary interactive color. Used for primary buttons and key interactive elements.

agentDocumentBlocks.purpose: Apply the primary text color for body content, headings, and labels on default background surfaces.

items.description: Use primary for default reading content; use secondary for supporting or reduced-emphasis text.
Use primary on default surfaces; use on-action inside filled interactive components.

items.guidance: Do not use on dark or colored background surfaces.
items.rationale: The token's contrast ratio is qualified against light surfaces only. On dark or saturated backgrounds it can drop below WCAG AA.

items.guidance: Do not override this token value at the component level.
items.rationale: An override forks the value from its source of truth: palette updates stop propagating and contrast audits no longer see the real value.

children.description: The default text color. Used for body text, headings, and labels on standard backgrounds.

agentDocumentBlocks.purpose: Apply the primary text color for body content, headings, and labels on default background surfaces.

items.description: Use primary for default reading content; use secondary for supporting or reduced-emphasis text.
Use primary on default surfaces; use on-action inside filled interactive components.

items.guidance: Do not use on dark or colored background surfaces.
items.rationale: The token's contrast ratio is qualified against light surfaces only. On dark or saturated backgrounds it can drop below WCAG AA.

items.guidance: Do not override this token value at the component level.
items.rationale: An override forks the value from its source of truth: palette updates stop propagating and contrast audits no longer see the real value.

children.description: Base spacing unit. The most commonly used spacing value across components and layouts.

agentDocumentBlocks.purpose: Apply the primary text color for body content, headings, and labels on default background surfaces.

items.description: Use primary for default reading content; use secondary for supporting or reduced-emphasis text.
Use primary on default surfaces; use on-action inside filled interactive components.

items.guidance: Do not use spacing tokens to set font sizes or icon dimensions.
items.rationale: Spacing, typography, and icon scales evolve independently. Cross-domain reuse means retuning one scale silently changes the others.

items.guidance: Do not override this token value at the component level.
items.rationale: An override forks the value from its source of truth and breaks the spacing rhythm the scale guarantees.

items.title: Intent
items.body: Define and organize a set of related design tokens, providing shared defaults for type and category.

items.guidance: Do not reference base palette tokens directly in component code.
items.rationale: Always reference semantic tokens that point to palette values.

entities.name: Spacing
entities.description: A spatial system built on a 4px base unit for consistent whitespace across all surfaces.

links.role: Provides all spatial tokens from space-0 through space-8.

items.title: Use the scale
items.description: Every spacing value must reference a token from the spacing scale. No hard-coded pixel values.

items.title: Density over decoration
items.description: Spacing exists to create clear relationships between elements, not to fill empty areas.

documentBlocks.name: Spacing Scale
documentBlocks.description: A geometric scale based on a 4px unit.

items.guidance: Components must not apply external margin. Margin between components is the responsibility of the parent layout.
items.rationale: Components that own their margins create unpredictable spacing when composed.

items.guidance: Use CSS gap as the primary mechanism for spacing between sibling elements.
items.rationale: Gap applies spacing uniformly without affecting the first or last child.

agentDocumentBlocks.purpose: Document spacing scale tokens and enforce their consistent application across layout, components, and typography.

items.description: Spacing governs whitespace and layout gaps; typography governs type size, weight, and line height.

items.guidance: Do not substitute spacing tokens with typography or icon-size tokens.
items.rationale: Spacing, typography, and icon scales evolve independently. Cross-domain reuse means retuning one scale silently changes the others.

items.guidance: Always use the next scale step up or down rather than custom values.
items.rationale: The scale exists to make spacing decisions consistent and finite. Custom values reintroduce the one-off decisions the scale was built to remove.

entities.name: Error Messaging
entities.description: How errors are surfaced through inline validation, summary banners, and toast notifications.

links.role: Page-level error summary listing all current errors.
Submit trigger that initiates client-side validation.

items.description: Client-side validation runs on all required fields. If any fail, submission is prevented.
Each invalid field displays an inline error message. An error summary alert appears at the top of the form with anchor links to each field.
The inline error for that field is removed. When all errors are resolved, the summary disappears.
When a form submission fails validation and the user needs to correct one or more fields.
When the feedback is a successful outcome.

alternative.rationale: Success feedback uses success colors and icons. Reusing the error pattern for positive outcomes confuses the visual language.

items.guidance: Always display inline errors directly below the invalid field.
items.rationale: Proximity eliminates the cognitive load of mapping an error message to its field.

items.guidance: Move focus to the error summary when it appears.
items.rationale: Screen reader users will not discover the summary unless focus is moved to it.

items.guidance: Do not clear the form or reset field values when validation fails.
items.rationale: Clearing the form destroys the user's work.

agentDocumentBlocks.purpose: Guide multi-component composition for surfacing, communicating, and resolving form validation errors.

items.description: Use error-messaging for validation and system errors; use empty-state for the absence of content.
Use error-messaging for form validation flows; use alert for non-form system or status messages.

items.guidance: Always include both an inline field error and a summary when multiple fields fail.
items.rationale: The summary gives screen reader users one announcement point and an error count; inline errors give sighted users fix-it-here proximity. Each alone strands one audience.

items.guidance: Do not use toast notifications as the sole error communication for form validation.
items.rationale: Toasts dismiss themselves and are not tied to the failing field. Once gone, the user has no path back to what went wrong.

items.guidance: Showing errors only on submit without inline field feedback.
items.rationale: Instead: Show inline errors on field blur and in a summary on submit.
